era: First Term Begins: Inauguration & First 100 Days (Jan–May 2017)

events.title: Trump Inaugurated as 45th President
events.detail: Donald J. Trump is sworn in as the 45th President of the United States on the steps of the Capitol. His inaugural address describes 'American carnage' and vows to put 'America First.' Crowd size controversy erupts immediately, with Press Secretary Sean Spicer claiming it was 'the largest audience ever.'

events.title: Women's March — Largest Single-Day Protest in US History
events.detail: An estimated 3–5 million people participate in the Women's March across the US and worldwide the day after Trump's inauguration, protesting his election and stated policy positions on women's rights, healthcare, and immigration. Sister marches occur in 600+ cities globally.

events.title: Executive Order 13769: First Travel Ban
events.detail: Trump signs Executive Order 13769, barring entry from seven Muslim-majority countries (Iran, Iraq, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, Yemen) for 90 days and suspending the refugee admissions program for 120 days. The order causes chaos at airports as travelers mid-flight are detained. Hundreds of lawyers rush to airports to offer pro bono assistance.

events.title: Federal Judge Blocks Travel Ban Nationwide
events.detail: US District Judge James Robart in Seattle issues a temporary restraining order blocking the travel ban nationwide, ruling that Washington State demonstrated harm and raised serious constitutional questions. The 9th Circuit Court of Appeals upholds the block days later. The Trump administration calls the ruling 'outrageous.'

sources.name: US District Court, Western District of Washington

events.title: National Security Advisor Michael Flynn Resigns
events.detail: Michael Flynn resigns as National Security Advisor after just 24 days in office, following revelations that he discussed US sanctions with Russian Ambassador Sergey Kislyak before Trump took office and misled Vice President Pence about those conversations. Flynn later pleads guilty to lying to the FBI as part of the Mueller investigation.

events.title: Neil Gorsuch Nominated to Supreme Court
events.detail: Trump nominates Neil Gorsuch to fill the Supreme Court seat left vacant by the death of Justice Antonin Scalia in February 2016. Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell had blocked President Obama's nominee Merrick Garland for nearly a year. Gorsuch is confirmed April 7, 2017 on a 54–45 vote after Republicans invoke the 'nuclear option' to end filibuster for SCOTUS nominees.

events.title: Revised Travel Ban (EO 13780) Signed
events.detail: Trump signs a revised travel ban removing Iraq from the list and clarifying exemptions for green card holders and existing visa holders. Federal courts in Hawaii and Maryland block this version too, ruling it discriminates on religious grounds. The Supreme Court eventually allows partial implementation in June 2017.

events.title: ACA Repeal Bill (AHCA) Pulled Before House Vote
events.detail: The American Health Care Act, the Republican plan to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act, is pulled from the House floor without a vote after Republicans lack sufficient support. Freedom Caucus members oppose it as insufficiently conservative while moderates fear coverage losses. Trump blames Democrats. 'Obamacare' survives.

events.title: FBI Director James Comey Fired
events.detail: Trump abruptly fires FBI Director James Comey, citing a recommendation from Deputy AG Rod Rosenstein over Comey's handling of the Clinton email investigation. Trump later tells NBC News he was thinking of 'this Russia thing' when he decided to fire Comey. The move triggers a political firestorm and accelerates calls for a special counsel.

events.title: Robert Mueller Appointed Special Counsel
events.detail: Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein appoints former FBI Director Robert Mueller as Special Counsel to investigate Russian interference in the 2016 election and any related matters. The investigation will last nearly two years, result in 37 indictments and 7 guilty pleas, and shape the remainder of Trump's first term.

events.title: Trump Withdraws US from Paris Climate Agreement
events.detail: Trump announces the US will withdraw from the Paris Climate Agreement, calling it a bad deal for American workers and an infringement on US sovereignty. The withdrawal process takes three years to complete. Trump promises to renegotiate better terms, though no renegotiation occurs. Over 200 US cities, states, and businesses pledge to uphold Paris goals anyway.

events.title: Supreme Court Allows Partial Travel Ban Implementation
events.detail: The Supreme Court partially reinstates the travel ban, allowing it to apply to foreign nationals who lack a 'bona fide relationship' with a US person or entity, but blocking it for those with such connections. The Court agrees to hear full arguments in October. The decision is seen as a partial victory for the administration.

events.title: Charlottesville 'Unite the Right' Rally — Heather Heyer Killed
events.detail: White supremacists and neo-Nazis rally in Charlottesville, Virginia ostensibly to protest removal of a Confederate statue. Counter-protester Heather Heyer is killed when a neo-Nazi drives a car into a crowd. Trump's response — saying there were 'very fine people on both sides' — draws widespread condemnation. Corporate CEOs resign from White House advisory councils.

events.title: Trump Ends DACA, Puts 700,000 'Dreamers' at Risk
events.detail: Attorney General Jeff Sessions announces the administration is rescinding DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals), the Obama-era program protecting ~700,000 undocumented immigrants who arrived as children. Trump gives Congress 6 months to act. Multiple courts later block the rescission; DACA remains in legal limbo for years. The Supreme Court ruled against the rescission in 2020.

events.title: Hurricane Maria Devastates Puerto Rico
events.detail: Category 4 Hurricane Maria makes direct landfall on Puerto Rico, causing catastrophic damage to the entire electrical grid. Puerto Rico's official death toll is later revised to nearly 3,000. Trump's federal response is widely criticized as inadequate; he visits 13 days later and tosses paper towels at survivors. The island remains without power for months in some areas.

events.title: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act Signed — $1.5T Cut
events.detail: Trump signs the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, the largest overhaul of the US tax code since 1986. The law cuts the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21%, doubles the standard deduction, and temporarily cuts individual rates. Republicans project economic growth; critics note benefits flow primarily to corporations and wealthy individuals and add $1.5 trillion to the deficit over 10 years.

events.title: Trump Recognizes Jerusalem as Israel's Capital
events.detail: Trump formally recognizes Jerusalem as Israel's capital and announces plans to move the US Embassy from Tel Aviv, breaking with decades of US foreign policy. The decision draws widespread international condemnation; the UN General Assembly passes a non-binding resolution 128-9 condemning it. The embassy move is completed in May 2018, coinciding with deadly protests in Gaza.

events.title: Manafort and Gates Indicted by Mueller Grand Jury
events.detail: Former Trump campaign chairman Paul Manafort and his deputy Rick Gates are indicted by a grand jury on 12 counts including money laundering, tax fraud, and conspiracy against the United States. Trump campaign foreign policy adviser George Papadopoulos pleads guilty on same day to lying to FBI about Russia contacts. Mueller investigation's first major public moves.

sources.name: Department of Justice / Special Counsel Office

events.title: NFL Anthem Kneeling Controversy Escalates
events.detail: After Trump calls for NFL owners to fire players who kneel during the national anthem, hundreds of players kneel or lock arms in solidarity during Week 3 games. The controversy re-ignites a national debate about race, police brutality, and patriotism that began when Colin Kaepernick first knelt in 2016. Trump continues to push the issue throughout his first term.

events.title: Supreme Court Upholds Third Travel Ban (Trump v. Hawaii)
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 5-4 in Trump v. Hawaii that the third version of the travel ban (Proclamation 9645, covering Chad, Iran, Libya, North Korea, Somalia, Syria, Venezuela, and Yemen) is a valid exercise of presidential authority over immigration. The ruling is seen as a major victory for Trump's executive power over immigration policy.

events.title: Trump Imposes 25% Steel, 10% Aluminum Tariffs
events.detail: Trump signs proclamations imposing 25% tariffs on steel imports and 10% on aluminum imports, citing national security concerns under Section 232. Canada, Mexico, and the EU are initially exempt, then hit with tariffs in June when negotiations stall. Trading partners retaliate with targeted tariffs on US exports. The move triggers the first major trade war of Trump's first term.

sources.name: Office of the US Trade Representative / Federal Register

events.title: 'Zero Tolerance' Family Separation Policy Implemented
events.detail: Attorney General Jeff Sessions announces a 'zero tolerance' policy for illegal border crossings, requiring prosecution of all adults — including asylum seekers — which separates children from parents. Over 2,500 children are separated. Photographs of children in cages and audio recordings of crying children cause a national and international outcry. Trump reverses the policy via executive order on June 20, 2018, but reunification is chaotic.

events.title: Singapore Summit — First US-North Korea Leader Meeting
events.detail: Trump meets North Korean leader Kim Jong Un in Singapore — the first-ever summit between sitting US and North Korean leaders. The two sign a vague joint statement on denuclearization. Trump calls it a historic achievement and announces suspension of US-South Korea military exercises. Critics note the statement contains no specifics, verification mechanisms, or timeline.

events.title: Trump Confronts NATO Allies, Questions Article 5 Commitment
events.detail: At the NATO summit in Brussels, Trump publicly berates Germany for its dependence on Russian natural gas and demands allies increase defense spending to 4% of GDP (double the 2% target). Behind closed doors, Trump reportedly threatens to withdraw from NATO and questions the alliance's Article 5 mutual defense commitment, alarming European leaders.

events.title: Helsinki Summit: Trump Sides with Putin Over US Intelligence
events.detail: At a joint press conference with Vladimir Putin in Helsinki, Trump says he sees no reason why Russia would have interfered in the 2016 election, contradicting unanimous conclusions of US intelligence agencies. The comment triggers a bipartisan firestorm; even many Republicans condemn it. Trump later claims he misspoke, saying he meant 'wouldn't' not 'would.' Former CIA Director Brennan calls it 'treasonous.'

events.title: Paul Manafort Convicted on 8 Counts of Financial Fraud
events.detail: Former Trump campaign chairman Paul Manafort is convicted by a jury in Virginia on eight counts of tax and bank fraud. He later pleads guilty in a separate DC case and cooperates briefly with Mueller before Mueller accuses him of lying. Trump calls Manafort a 'brave man' and repeatedly refuses to rule out a pardon. Trump eventually pardons Manafort in December 2020.

sources.name: US District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia

events.title: Jamal Khashoggi Murdered in Saudi Consulate
events.detail: Washington Post journalist and Saudi dissident Jamal Khashoggi is murdered and dismembered inside the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, Turkey. CIA concludes Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman ordered the killing. Trump refuses to impose severe sanctions on Saudi Arabia, saying 'maybe he did and maybe he didn't' about MBS and citing Saudi arms deals and oil. The response draws bipartisan condemnation.

events.title: Midterms: Democrats Recapture House, Republicans Hold Senate
events.detail: Democrats win a net 40 seats in the House of Representatives — the 'blue wave' — flipping it to Democratic control for the first time since 2010. Nancy Pelosi becomes Speaker again. Republicans gain 2 Senate seats. The election sets up two years of divided government and lays the groundwork for investigations into Trump by House committees. Trump fires Sessions the following day.

events.title: Government Shutdown Begins Over Border Wall Funding
events.detail: Trump refuses to sign a continuing resolution without $5.7 billion for a border wall; Democrats refuse to fund it. The partial government shutdown begins on December 22, 2018. It becomes the longest government shutdown in US history at 35 days. 800,000 federal workers go without pay; air traffic controllers, TSA agents, and Coast Guard members work without paychecks. Ends January 25, 2019 without wall funding.

sources.name: Office of Management and Budget / Congressional Record

events.title: US-China Trade War: Tariffs Escalate to $250B in Goods
events.detail: The US-China trade war escalates through 2018. The US imposes 25% tariffs on $34 billion of Chinese goods (July), then $16 billion more (August), then 10% on $200 billion more (September). China retaliates with tariffs on US agricultural products, automobiles, and other goods. American farmers are hit particularly hard; the administration creates a $12 billion aid package for them.

sources.name: Office of the US Trade Representative

events.title: Longest Shutdown Ends After 35 Days — No Wall Funding
events.detail: Trump agrees to reopen the government for three weeks without any wall funding after growing pressure from air travel disruptions (FAA staffing shortages), federal workers missing second paychecks, and polling showing public blame falling on him. He later declares a national emergency to redirect military construction funds to border wall — challenged successfully in lower courts, partially upheld by SCOTUS.

events.title: Hanoi Summit Collapses — No Deal with North Korea
events.detail: Trump and Kim Jong Un meet in Hanoi, Vietnam for a second summit. Negotiations break down over North Korea's demand for comprehensive sanctions relief in exchange for dismantling only part of its nuclear program. Trump walks away without signing any agreement. The collapse marks the effective end of US-North Korea diplomatic progress during Trump's first term.

events.title: Mueller Report Released — Obstruction Not Exonerated
events.detail: Special Counsel Robert Mueller's 448-page report is released (with redactions). Volume I finds insufficient evidence of criminal conspiracy between the Trump campaign and Russia. Volume II documents 10 potential instances of obstruction of justice but declines to reach a prosecutorial judgment. Mueller says the report 'does not exonerate' Trump on obstruction. AG Barr's summary had characterized it more favorably to Trump. Trump declares 'total exoneration.'

events.title: Trump Tweets 'Go Back' at AOC and the Squad
events.detail: Trump tweets that four Democratic congresswomen of color (Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, Ilhan Omar, Ayanna Pressley, Rashida Tlaib) — all US citizens, three born in the US — should 'go back' to 'the totally broken and crime infested places from which they came.' The tweets are widely condemned as racist. The House votes to formally condemn the tweets, with 4 Republicans joining Democrats.

events.title: El Paso Walmart Mass Shooting — 23 Killed
events.detail: A gunman targeting Hispanics kills 23 people at a Walmart in El Paso, Texas. The shooter's manifesto echoes Trump's rhetoric about a 'Hispanic invasion.' Democrats and some Republicans call on Trump to tone down his immigration language. Trump visits El Paso but is met with protests. No significant gun legislation passes Congress in the aftermath.

events.title: Ukraine Phone Call — Whistleblower Complaint Filed
events.detail: Trump calls Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky and, according to the call summary, asks him to investigate Biden family dealings with Ukraine and the 2016 election — implying US military aid may be conditioned on cooperation. A CIA officer files a whistleblower complaint, calling it 'deeply disturbing.' The call summary is released September 25, 2019. The incident triggers the first impeachment.

sources.name: White House call transcript / Director of National Intelligence

events.title: House Formally Launches Impeachment Inquiry
events.detail: Speaker Nancy Pelosi announces a formal impeachment inquiry following the Ukraine whistleblower revelations. House committees subpoena State Department documents and witnesses. The White House initially refuses to cooperate, calling it a 'partisan witch hunt.' Public hearings before the Intelligence Committee begin in November, featuring testimony from Ambassador Bill Taylor, Lt. Col. Alexander Vindman, and Ambassador Gordon Sondland.

events.title: House Impeaches Trump on Abuse of Power & Obstruction
events.detail: The House of Representatives impeaches Trump on two articles: abuse of power (230-197) and obstruction of Congress (229-198). No Republicans vote for impeachment; two Democrats vote against. Trump sends a fiery letter to Speaker Pelosi calling the proceedings 'an unprecedented and unconstitutional abuse of power.' Speaker Pelosi holds the articles for nearly a month before transmitting to the Senate.

sources.name: US House of Representatives / Congressional Record

events.title: General Qasem Soleimani Killed in US Drone Strike
events.detail: A US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport kills Iranian General Qasem Soleimani, commander of the IRGC Quds Force, along with Iraqi militia leader Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis. Trump says Soleimani was planning imminent attacks on US personnel. Iran retaliates with ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq, injuring dozens of US troops with traumatic brain injuries. The strike raises fears of wider war.

sources.name: US Department of Defense / White House

events.title: Senate Acquits Trump on Both Impeachment Articles
events.detail: The Senate acquits Trump 52-48 on abuse of power (with Republican Senator Mitt Romney voting guilty — the first senator in US history to vote to convict a president of their own party) and 53-47 on obstruction of Congress. All Democrats vote guilty on both counts. Trump's approval rating remains steady throughout. Trump delivers a State of the Union the day before, refusing to shake Pelosi's hand; she tears up his speech.

events.title: WHO Declares COVID-19 Pandemic; Trump Declares Emergency
events.detail: The WHO declares COVID-19 a global pandemic. Trump declares a national emergency March 13, releasing $50 billion in federal aid. Trump initially downplays the threat ('under control', 'hoax', 15 cases going to zero), then restricts travel from Europe. The pandemic dominates the final year of his first term. Operation Warp Speed is launched in May 2020, producing authorized vaccines by December.

events.title: CARES Act Signed — $2.2 Trillion COVID Stimulus
events.detail: Trump signs the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security (CARES) Act, a $2.2 trillion stimulus package — the largest in US history at that time. It includes $1,200 direct payments to individuals, $600/week enhanced unemployment benefits, $500 billion in loans to corporations, and $349 billion in Paycheck Protection Program loans for small businesses. Congress passes it unanimously in the Senate.

events.title: George Floyd Murder Sparks Nationwide BLM Protests
events.detail: Minneapolis police officer Derek Chauvin kills George Floyd by kneeling on his neck for over 9 minutes. Nationwide protests erupt — the largest wave of civil unrest since 1968. Trump threatens to invoke the Insurrection Act, tweets 'when the looting starts, the shooting starts,' and calls for governors to 'dominate' protesters. Statues of Confederate leaders and others are removed across the country.

events.title: Lafayette Square Cleared for Trump Church Photo Op
events.detail: Law enforcement using tear gas, batons, and flash grenades clear peaceful protesters from Lafayette Square in front of the White House so Trump can walk to St. John's Church holding a Bible. The move draws widespread condemnation, including from Defense Secretary Esper, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs Milley (who later publicly apologizes for appearing in the walk), and former Defense Secretary Mattis.

sources.name: US Park Police / DOI Inspector General

events.title: Abraham Accords: UAE and Bahrain Normalize with Israel
events.detail: The United Arab Emirates and Bahrain sign normalization agreements with Israel at the White House — the first Arab-Israeli peace deals in 25 years. Sudan and Morocco follow in subsequent months. Brokered by Trump son-in-law Jared Kushner. Palestinians condemn the accords as a betrayal; critics note Iran hawks benefit as Gulf states and Israel align against Tehran.

events.title: Justice Ginsburg Dies; Trump Nominates Barrett 8 Days Later
events.detail: Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg dies of pancreatic cancer at age 87. Trump nominates federal judge Amy Coney Barrett to fill the seat just 8 days later, 46 days before the election. Democrats protest that McConnell blocked Garland's nomination in 2016 because of the election. Republicans confirm Barrett 52-48 on October 26, one week before Election Day — securing a 6-3 conservative SCOTUS majority.

events.title: Trump Tests Positive for COVID-19, Hospitalized
events.detail: Trump announces he has tested positive for COVID-19 and is hospitalized at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center for three days. He receives experimental monoclonal antibody treatment, steroids, and remdesivir. He dramatically returns to the White House, removes his mask on the balcony while still infectious. He continues to hold rallies shortly after recovery, calling himself 'immune.'

sources.name: White House / Walter Reed Medical Center

events.title: Biden Wins 2020 Presidential Election
events.detail: Major news outlets call the 2020 presidential election for Joe Biden, who wins with 306 electoral votes to Trump's 232 and a 7-million-vote popular vote margin. Biden wins Pennsylvania, Wisconsin, Michigan, Arizona, and Georgia — all states Trump won in 2016. Trump refuses to concede, claims the election was stolen through widespread fraud, and launches legal battles in multiple states.

events.title: 'Stop the Steal' Campaign: 60+ Legal Challenges Fail
events.detail: Trump and his allies file over 60 legal challenges to the election results across multiple states. All are rejected by courts — including judges appointed by Trump — for lack of evidence of fraud. The Supreme Court rejects a Texas lawsuit that asked to throw out votes in four swing states (December 11, 2020). AG Bill Barr says DOJ finds no evidence of fraud sufficient to change the result; he resigns December 23.

events.title: Trump Pressures Georgia SOS Raffensperger to 'Find' Votes
events.detail: In a recorded phone call, Trump asks Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger to 'find 11,780 votes' — exactly one more than Biden's margin — to flip the state. Raffensperger refuses. The call is later cited as a key piece of evidence in the Georgia RICO indictment against Trump (2023) and in his second impeachment proceedings. Trump describes the call as 'perfect.'

sources.name: Georgia Secretary of State Office (recorded call)

events.title: Trump Rally at Ellipse: 'Fight Like Hell' Speech
events.detail: At a rally on the Ellipse near the White House, Trump and his allies give fiery speeches urging supporters to march to the Capitol to stop Congress from certifying Biden's victory. Trump says 'if you don't fight like hell you're not going to have a country anymore' and promises to march with them. He does not go to the Capitol but watches events on TV for hours before issuing a delayed, tepid plea to leave.

events.title: Capitol Attack: Mob Breaches Congress, Halts Certification
events.detail: Thousands of Trump supporters storm the US Capitol, breaching the building and forcing members of Congress to shelter in place or evacuate. The certification of the Electoral College vote is halted for hours. 5 people die in connection with the attack; 140 police officers are injured. Over 1,000 rioters are eventually charged. The event is described by the Jan. 6 Select Committee as the result of a coordinated effort to prevent the peaceful transfer of power.

sources.name: US Capitol Police / January 6 Select Committee

events.title: House Impeaches Trump a Second Time — 10 Republicans Vote Yes
events.detail: The House impeaches Trump for 'incitement of insurrection' on a 232-197 vote, with 10 Republicans joining all Democrats — the most bipartisan impeachment in US history. House Republican Conference Chair Liz Cheney votes yes, calling it 'the greatest betrayal by a president of the United States of his office and his oath.' The Senate trial begins after Biden's inauguration; Trump is acquitted 57-43 (conviction requires 67).

sources.name: US House of Representatives / Congressional Record

events.title: FDA Authorizes First COVID Vaccine — Operation Warp Speed
events.detail: The FDA grants Emergency Use Authorization to the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine, followed a week later by Moderna's. Operation Warp Speed, the Trump administration's public-private partnership to accelerate vaccine development, is credited with helping compress the typical multi-year vaccine timeline to under 12 months. Over 4 million doses are administered in the first month. The Moderna and J&J vaccines follow.

events.title: Senate Acquits Trump on Second Impeachment 57-43
events.detail: The Senate votes 57-43 to convict Trump on incitement of insurrection — the most bipartisan vote to convict a president, but 10 short of the two-thirds majority required. 7 Republicans join all Democrats in voting guilty: Collins, Murkowski, Romney, Sasse, Toomey, Cassidy, and Burr. McConnell votes to acquit but gives a searing floor speech blaming Trump for the Capitol attack. Trump calls it a 'witch hunt.'

events.title: FBI Searches Mar-a-Lago for Classified Documents
events.detail: FBI agents execute a search warrant at Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida, seizing approximately 11,000 documents including roughly 300 classified documents. The search follows months of negotiations after the National Archives retrieved 15 boxes in January 2022 and referred the matter to DOJ. Trump calls it a politically motivated 'raid.' It eventually leads to federal charges in June 2023.

events.title: Trump Announces 2024 Presidential Campaign
events.detail: Two days after midterm elections in which Republican candidates underperformed expectations, Trump announces his 2024 presidential campaign at Mar-a-Lago — earlier than any previous major-party front-runner. His announcement is overshadowed by poor Republican midterm performance, and many party leaders distance themselves. Florida Governor Ron DeSantis is seen as the main rival for the nomination but ultimately drops out in January 2024.

events.title: Federal Indictment: 37 Counts Over Classified Documents
events.detail: A federal grand jury in Miami indicts Trump on 37 counts related to his retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago and obstruction of DOJ attempts to retrieve them. Charges include willful retention of national defense information, conspiracy to obstruct justice, and making false statements. Trump pleads not guilty. The case is assigned to Trump-appointed Judge Aileen Cannon, who later dismisses it citing appointment challenges to Special Counsel Jack Smith.

events.title: Federal Jan. 6 Indictment: 4 Counts Including Conspiracy
events.detail: Special Counsel Jack Smith indicts Trump on four federal counts related to efforts to overturn the 2020 election: conspiracy to defraud the United States, obstruction of Congress, conspiracy to obstruct Congress, and deprivation of civil rights. Trump pleads not guilty. The case is stayed pending SCOTUS's immunity ruling, which significantly narrows it. Smith drops the case after Trump's 2024 victory per DOJ policy.

events.title: Georgia RICO Indictment: Trump and 18 Co-Defendants
events.detail: Fulton County DA Fani Willis indicts Trump and 18 co-defendants under Georgia's RICO statute for a criminal enterprise to overturn Georgia's 2020 election results. The 98-page indictment details 161 acts, including the Raffensperger call, fake elector schemes, and unauthorized access to election equipment in Coffee County. Trump pleads not guilty. The case faces delays due to co-defendant trials and appeals; as of early 2026 it is on pause.

events.title: Supreme Court: Presidents Have Broad Immunity for Official Acts
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 6-3 in Trump v. United States that former presidents have absolute immunity for core constitutional acts and presumptive immunity for official acts. The ruling sends the Jan. 6 federal case back to the lower court to determine which charges involve official vs. unofficial acts. Legal scholars call it a landmark expansion of executive power; dissenters warn it places the president 'above the law.'

events.title: Assassination Attempt at Butler, PA Rally
events.detail: A 20-year-old gunman opens fire at a Trump campaign rally in Butler, Pennsylvania. Trump is struck by a bullet grazing his right ear and raises his fist as Secret Service surrounds him in a widely circulated image. One rally attendee is killed and two others critically wounded. The gunman is killed by Secret Service. A second assassination attempt occurs September 15 in West Palm Beach, Florida, where a rifle-armed man is apprehended at a golf course.

events.title: Biden Drops Out, Harris Becomes Democratic Nominee
events.detail: After a disastrous June 27 debate performance and growing Democratic pressure, President Biden announces he will not seek re-election and endorses Vice President Kamala Harris. Harris quickly secures enough delegate support and is formally nominated at the Democratic National Convention in August. She selects Minnesota Governor Tim Walz as running mate. Trump's general election opponent shifts from Biden to Harris.

events.title: Trump Convicted on 34 Felony Counts in Hush Money Trial
events.detail: A Manhattan jury convicts Trump on all 34 felony counts of falsifying business records related to hush money payments to adult film actress Stormy Daniels before the 2016 election. Trump is the first US president, current or former, to be convicted of a felony crime. He is sentenced in January 2025 to an unconditional discharge — no prison or probation — in part because he is president-elect.

sources.name: Manhattan District Attorney / New York Supreme Court

events.title: Trump Wins 2024 Election — First to Win Non-Consecutive Terms
events.detail: Trump defeats Kamala Harris decisively, winning 312 electoral votes to Harris's 226 and becoming the first president since Grover Cleveland to win non-consecutive presidential terms. Republicans also retake the Senate and hold the House. Trump sweeps the 'blue wall' states of Pennsylvania, Michigan, and Wisconsin. He also wins the popular vote — the first Republican to do so since George W. Bush in 2004.

era: Second Term: Day One & First Month (Jan–Feb 2025)

events.title: Trump Inaugurated as 47th President
events.detail: Trump is sworn in as the 47th President — the first president to serve non-consecutive terms since Grover Cleveland. The ceremony is held inside the Capitol Rotunda due to cold weather. Trump delivers a lengthy address promising a 'golden age of America.' He is joined by a who's-who of tech billionaires including Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, and Mark Zuckerberg.

events.title: Trump Signs ~30 Executive Orders on Day One
events.detail: On his first day, Trump signs a record-setting blitz of executive orders including: declaring a national emergency at the southern border, invoking the Alien Enemies Act (1798), ending birthright citizenship for children of undocumented immigrants (later blocked by courts), withdrawing from the Paris Climate Agreement, withdrawing from the WHO, reversing Biden's DEI requirements, renaming the Gulf of Mexico to 'Gulf of America,' and pardoning January 6 defendants.

events.title: Trump Issues Blanket Pardon for 1,500+ Jan. 6 Defendants
events.detail: Within hours of his inauguration, Trump issues pardons for all January 6 defendants, including those convicted of violent offenses against police officers. Over 1,500 people who had been charged or convicted are released or have their convictions wiped. Critics including law enforcement groups and some Republicans call it a dangerous signal; supporters call it correcting politically motivated prosecutions.

events.title: DOGE Established — Musk Leads Government Efficiency Push
events.detail: Trump establishes the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) — not an official Cabinet department but an advisory body — and names Elon Musk as its head. DOGE gains access to federal payment systems and data, leading to mass firings of federal workers, contract cancellations, and agency closures. The office operates with minimal transparency, sparking numerous legal challenges. DOGE's claimed savings figures are widely disputed by budget analysts.

events.title: Trump Reverses TikTok Ban, Orders 90-Day Pause
events.detail: Hours after the law requiring ByteDance to divest TikTok or face a ban took effect (January 19), Trump issues an executive order directing DOJ not to enforce the ban for 90 days, giving time for a potential deal. TikTok briefly went dark before coming back online. Trump says he wants the US to receive a 50% ownership stake in TikTok. Negotiations proceed slowly through 2025.

events.title: Birthright Citizenship EO Immediately Blocked by Courts
events.detail: Trump signs an executive order attempting to end birthright citizenship for children born in the US to undocumented immigrants or those on temporary visas, reinterpreting the 14th Amendment. Within days, multiple federal judges issue injunctions blocking the order, ruling it unconstitutional on its face. The legal battle heads to the Supreme Court; lower courts unanimously rule against the administration.

events.title: DOGE Effectively Shutters USAID
events.detail: The Trump administration, through DOGE, moves to dismantle the US Agency for International Development (USAID), placing most staff on administrative leave, closing its Washington headquarters, and cancelling thousands of foreign aid contracts. Secretary of State Rubio is named acting USAID administrator. The dismantling halts humanitarian aid programs in dozens of countries, triggering warnings of humanitarian crises and legal challenges from contractors.

events.title: 25% Tariffs Imposed on Canada and Mexico
events.detail: Trump imposes 25% tariffs on all imports from Canada and Mexico and 10% on Canadian energy, citing fentanyl flows and illegal immigration. Canada announces retaliatory tariffs on $155 billion CAD in US goods. Mexico announces targeted retaliatory tariffs. The move roils markets and triggers crisis talks; a brief pause is announced weeks later after both countries take some border steps. Tariffs resume and remain in dispute.

events.title: DOGE Offers 'Fork in Road' Buyout to 2.3M Federal Workers
events.detail: DOGE sends federal employees an email (dubbed 'deferred resignation' offer) giving them until February 6 to accept 8 months of pay to resign voluntarily. Approximately 75,000 federal workers initially accept. Courts issue injunctions blocking the administration from firing those who don't accept. The program is repeatedly challenged, as courts question whether the administration can unilaterally offer such buyouts without Congressional authorization.

sources.name: Office of Personnel Management / White House

events.title: Additional 10% Tariff on China Takes Effect
events.detail: An additional 10% tariff on all Chinese imports takes effect, building on existing tariffs from Trump's first term. China retaliates by imposing tariffs on US agricultural products and restricting exports of critical minerals. This is the opening move in what becomes an escalating tariff war culminating in 145% tariffs on Chinese goods by April 2025. The US-China trade war escalates significantly beyond first-term levels.

sources.name: Office of the US Trade Representative

era: Second Term: Liberation Day Tariffs & Global Trade War (Apr 2025–)

events.title: Alien Enemies Act Invoked to Deport Venezuelan Gang Members
events.detail: Trump invokes the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 — last used in World War II — to declare Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua an invading force and bypass normal deportation procedures. Hundreds of Venezuelans are deported to El Salvador's CECOT mega-prison under a deal with President Nayib Bukele. Courts issue emergency stays; the Supreme Court allows removals to continue while litigation proceeds. Critics say due process is violated.

events.title: Federal DEI Programs Eliminated Across Government
events.detail: Executive orders ban diversity, equity, and inclusion programs across the federal government, direct agencies to investigate private-sector DEI programs as potential civil rights violations, and require contractors to certify they don't have DEI programs. Thousands of federal DEI officers are placed on leave. Multiple universities and corporations preemptively scale back DEI initiatives. Multiple lawsuits challenge aspects of the orders.

events.title: 'Liberation Day': Trump Announces Sweeping Global Tariffs
events.detail: Trump unveils a sweeping set of 'reciprocal' tariffs on virtually all US trading partners in a Rose Garden ceremony on what he dubs 'Liberation Day.' The plan includes a 10% baseline tariff on all imports, with higher rates for specific countries: 34% on China (on top of existing), 20% on EU, 24% on Japan, 25% on South Korea. Markets plunge sharply in response. The announcement triggers the most acute financial market volatility since 2020.

events.title: China Tariffs Escalate to 145% as Trade War Peaks
events.detail: After China retaliates with 125% tariffs on US goods, the US escalates tariffs on Chinese imports to 145% — effectively cutting off most trade between the two largest economies. China suspends rare earth mineral exports critical to US defense and technology industries. Global supply chains are severely disrupted. US consumer goods prices begin rising. The tariff war is the most severe in over 90 years.

events.title: Trump Announces 90-Day Pause on Tariffs (Except China)
events.detail: One week after Liberation Day, Trump announces a 90-day pause on the new reciprocal tariffs for most countries (reverted to 10% baseline) while trade negotiations proceed, citing market volatility and concerns from treasury bond markets. China is explicitly excluded and tariffs continue to escalate. The announcement triggers the largest single-day Dow Jones gain since 2020. Critics see the whipsaw as policy incoherence; supporters say it's strategic leverage.

events.title: DOGE Cuts: Education Dept, NOAA, EPA Face Mass Layoffs
events.detail: DOGE oversees mass reductions across federal agencies including the Department of Education (proposed for elimination), NOAA (hundreds of meteorologists fired, raising safety concerns), EPA (climate research defunded), IRS (10,000+ jobs cut), CFPB (effectively frozen), and others. Federal courts issue numerous injunctions blocking specific firings. The administration argues Congress authorized executive restructuring; opponents say only Congress can eliminate agencies.

sources.name: Office of Personnel Management / Congressional Budget Office

events.title: Trump Demands Control of Greenland and Calls Canada '51st State'
events.detail: Trump repeatedly and publicly expresses desire to acquire Greenland from Denmark for national security reasons, refuses to rule out military or economic coercion, and says he will impose tariffs on Denmark/EU if they resist. He also calls Canada the '51st state' and imposes tariffs referencing its trade surplus. Both NATO allies protest loudly. Greenland's parliament unanimously rejects annexation. The rhetoric strains transatlantic relations.

events.title: Trump Pushes Ukraine-Russia Ceasefire Talks
events.detail: Trump moves quickly to broker a ceasefire in Ukraine, meeting with Zelensky at the White House (a contentious Oval Office meeting where Trump and Vance berate Zelensky on camera) and engaging with Putin. Trump suspends US military aid to Ukraine temporarily to pressure Zelensky to negotiate. A proposed ceasefire framework stalls over territorial concessions. European allies are alarmed; UK and France increase their own support.

events.title: Operation Aurora: Mass ICE Deportation Raids Nationwide
events.detail: ICE conducts large-scale deportation raids in cities across the US, including so-called 'sanctuary cities.' The administration deploys military personnel in support roles at the border and interior. Military flights transport deportees to home countries; the administration pays El Salvador, Honduras, and others to accept deportees. Overall deportations in Trump's second term's first months surpass any comparable period in US history.

events.title: S&P 500 Falls ~15% from Inauguration Peak on Tariff Fears
events.detail: US stock markets experience sharp volatility tied to escalating tariffs and trade war uncertainty. After reaching new highs following Trump's November 2024 election win, the S&P 500 falls approximately 15% from its peak by early April 2025. The bond market also shows stress with rising 10-year Treasury yields. The 90-day tariff pause on April 9 triggers a large rebound, but markets remain volatile as trade negotiations continue.
